feedback.text: Dear European Commission, I would like to draw your attention to some possible issues in the context of video games. As a consumer of digital products such as computer applications and video games, I often engage in uncomfortable selling practices. I would also like to mention the various electronic stores that try to sell only one licence instead of an electronic product. This already promotes this software as purchases to the supplier such as Steam or Sony' in your stores. However, their general terms and conditions define this in a rather opaque way and I do not buy a product. A licence is not apparent at the time of purchase (Playstation Store, Steam). Once the problem is that of copyright, and I, for my private use, become the owner of the software (personal opinion). Another problem with these licences is the manufacturer’s ability to remove licences from buyers’ accounts. See The Crew of Ubisoft. However, there is no fixed expiry date for these licences. This allows the licence to be annulled whenever the manufacturer feels thereon (see Ubisoft the crew). This is often done years after the expiry of the legal guarantee. Some manufacturers, such as Nintendo, also do not see the need to discount their own games in your electronic stores. This means that some games can be bought at a lower price in the local retail sector, but once physics production stops paying very expensive digital prices. However, some video games I want to buy as a single title can only be purchased as a subscription. I pay EUR 40 per year for a single title. After five years, I lost EUR 200 or lose access (see Nintendo Switch Online). However, these video games cannot be purchased anywhere else, as they are so-called exclusive titles. These exclusive titles belong to the distributor of Abo’s Sprich Nintendo itself. Even Microsoft Office or films own purchase versions that are not linked to a subscription. An increased online use of video games leads to planned obsolescence. When switching off verification servers, for example, Kingdom Hearts (Steam, Nintendo E-Shop ‘Stream Edition’) for permanent loss of access. These games often refuse access without a fixed internet connection. However, this internet connection is often not necessary. If I die now or a digital estate inherits, I do not allow me to transfer these licences online. Reference is made to the ABGs (see Steam). Pre-all mobile games on mobile phones own gambling mechanisms. This is often exploited to generate millions of profits, but if manufacturers think that they will no longer be profitable, these games will be switched off. Consumers are encouraged to do so in the hope of drawing 10, 20 to amounts larger than EUR 1000 for these gambling mechanisms. But if these games are shut down, access to these purchases and related features is denied (e.g. Bandai: Namco Tales of Link, Tales of the Rays, Tales of Crestoria,). It does not require the manufacturer to receive the servers, but at least one functional offline access after the end of such life service Games would be desirable. I hope this feedback will look at some of these sales practices in more detail.
